root_cause.text: The code performed an expensive certificate chain cleaning operation before checking if certificate pinning was actually required for the given hostname, causing unnecessary processing overhead.
root_cause.evidence: The patch moves the `new CertificateChainCleaner(trustRootIndex).clean(peerCertificates)` call to occur after `findMatchingPins(hostname)` and the `if (pins == null) return;` check, as indicated by the commit message "Don't pay to clean certificates if we aren't pinning."

agent_logs.root_cause_candidate: The code performed an expensive certificate chain cleaning operation before checking if certificate pinning was actually required for the given hostname, causing unnecessary processing overhead.
agent_logs.evidence_points: The patch moves the `new CertificateChainCleaner(trustRootIndex).clean(peerCertificates)` call to occur after `findMatchingPins(hostname)` and the `if (pins == null) return;` check, as indicated by the commit message "Don't pay to clean certificates if we aren't pinning."

intent_record.intent_summary: Defer certificate chain cleaning until after confirming matching pins exist to optimize performance.
intent_record.what: Moved the certificate chain cleaning logic to occur after checking if there are matching pins.
intent_record.how: Prevents unnecessary certificate chain cleaning if the hostname has no matching pins, avoiding unnecessary computation.

grouping_result.intent: Defer certificate chain cleaning until after confirming matching pins exist to optimize performance.

relevance_review.reason: The group's intent to defer certificate chain cleaning until after checking for matching pins directly addresses the root cause of the vulnerability, which is the unnecessary overhead caused by cleaning before checking for pins.

candidates.rationale: This statement performs the expensive certificate chain cleaning operation before checking if certificate pinning is required for the hostname.

vuln_statements.explanation: This statement unconditionally performs the expensive certificate chain cleaning operation before checking if certificate pinning is required for the hostname, causing unnecessary processing overhead.

bic_trace_step.commit_msg:
Don't build a trust root index on Android.

We can just cheat and use reflection to use Conscrypt's trust root index
dir
bic_trace_step.code_snippet:
commit 784fabac7d1586a5614bd4bc8854fd62850dbe26
Author: jwilson <jwilson@squareup.com>
Date:   Sun Feb 7 11:09:38 2016 -0500

    Don't build a trust root index on Android.
    
    We can just cheat and use reflection to use Conscrypt's trust root index
    directly. This results in a substantial savings in app startup - 500
    milliseconds or more.
    
    Closes: https://github.com/square/okhttp/issues/2321

diff --git a/okhttp/src/main/java/okhttp3/CertificatePinner.java b/okhttp/src/main/
bic_trace_step.reason: The 'check' method performs the expensive certificate chain cleaning operation (via 'new CertificateChainCleaner(trustRootIndex).clean(peerCertificates)') before checking if there are actually any pins for the given hostname ('findMatchingPins(hostname)'). This matches the vulnerability root cause.

bic_trace_step.commit_msg:
Start using CertificateAuthorityCouncil in CertificatePinner.

The wiring is definitely a bit more awkward than I would
bic_trace_step.code_snippet:
commit e121ed1ad291cbe04de989683d23f549d8e305db
Author: jwilson <jwilson@squareup.com>
Date:   Mon Feb 1 20:02:07 2016 -0500

    Start using CertificateAuthorityCouncil in CertificatePinner.
    
    The wiring is definitely a bit more awkward than I would like. The
    CertificateAuthorityCountil is a non-public class, and this change
    sneaks an instance into the CertificatePinner that couples it to
    the OkHttp client's SSLSocketFactory.
    
    A nicer solution is to expose Certificate
bic_trace_step.reason: The commit introduces the vulnerable logic by calling 'certificateAuthorityCouncil.normalizeCertificateChain(peerCertificates)' before calling 'findMatchingPins(hostname)'. This means the expensive certificate chain cleaning operation is performed even if no pins are configured for the hostname.

The function does not contain any certificate chain cleaning logic, which is the core of the vulnerability described in the root cause. It simply checks if pins exist and verifies them against the provided peer certificates.
